---
title: "Methods"
---

## Research question

How do savory food intake and cholesterol jointly predict the presence of key beneficial gut microbial taxa?

## Data source

The project uses data from *Gut microbiome signatures of vegan, vegetarian and omnivore diets and associated health outcomes across 21,561 individuals*, representing a subset of the ZOE PREDICT 1 (P1) cohort in the United Kingdom.

- Working cohort size: `N = 92` participants
- Inputs: dietary pattern metrics, clinical biomarkers, and microbiome taxonomic data
- Data format: three source `.xlsx` files merged into a unified analysis matrix

## Analytical pipeline

1. **Cohort and data assembly**  
   Construct a working analysis dataset from ZOE PREDICT 1 subset data plus reference public metagenomic information.

2. **Data integration and cleaning**  
   Merge all files into `feature_matrix.csv` to simplify analysis in Python and R.

3. **Taxa binarization**  
   Convert taxonomy strings into per-taxon presence/absence indicators.

4. **Distribution checks**  
   Generate Q-Q plots in R to assess normality and identify skewed variables.

5. **Exploratory associations**  
   Build correlation heatmaps (`corrplot`) and run PCA to identify dominant features spanning diet, biomarkers, and taxa.

6. **Statistical modeling**  
   Fit logistic regression models for target taxa using savory intake, cholesterol, and their interaction while adjusting for age, sex, and BMI.

7. **Multiple testing correction**  
   Apply Benjamini-Hochberg FDR control across model families.

## References and tooling

- [ZOE PREDICT 1 Study](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41591-020-1145-4)
- [Vegan/Vegetarian/Omnivore Study](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41564-024-01870-z)
- [R `corrplot` package](https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/corrplot/vignettes/corrplot-intro.html)
- [Benjamini-Hochberg FDR paper](https://www.jstor.org/stable/2346101)
